perturbation.original_text: WHEREAS , Seller is engaged in the business of designing, marketing, importing, selling and distributing various types of bedding, blanket and bath products and related accessories for the Infant and Toddler Retail Market (as defined herein) through Seller’s unincorporated baby product line (the “ Business ”);
perturbation.changed_text: WHEREAS , Seller is engaged in business activities;
perturbation.explanation: The removal of specific details about Seller's business (designing, marketing, etc.) and replacing it with a general term ('business activities') creates uncertainty. Later sections referencing the 'Business' as specifically defined become vague. This contradicts the initial intention of precise definition as other sections use the term with the original specificity in mind.
perturbation.contradicted_text: (c) “ Confidential Information ” shall mean all customer and supplier lists, marketing arrangements, business plans, projections, financial information, training manuals, pricing manuals, product development plans, market strategies, internal performance statistics and other competitively sensitive information of Seller used solely in the Business and not generally known by the public, whether or not in written or tangible form. Notwithstanding the foregoing, the definition of Confidential Information shall not include any of the foregoing items insofar as they relate to Seller Exclusive Products.
perturbation.justification: The original text defines the Seller's business with specificity, while the changed text replaces this with a general term 'business activities'. The contradicted text defines 'Confidential Information' as information used solely in the 'Business', which was initially defined with specific details. The change to 'business activities' renders the definition of 'Confidential Information' vague, as it is no longer clear which activities the information must relate to, creating a contradiction. This aligns with the Omissions-In Text Contradiction definition.

perturbation.original_text: (b) “ Competitive Products ” shall mean the type of products designed, marketed, imported, and sold or distributed by Seller to the Infant and Toddler Retail Market in connection with the operation of the Business prior to the date hereof (which shall not include any Seller Exclusive Products (as hereinafter defined)) without regard to (i) the prices at which such products may be sold, (ii) any tradenames, trademarks, brands, labels, logos or other identifying characteristics used in selling such products, or (iii) the types of businesses within the Infant and Toddler Retail Market that may purchase such products.
perturbation.explanation: By omitting the detailed description of 'Competitive Products,' the contract fails to specify the types of products covered by the non-compete clause. Later clauses referencing 'Competitive Products' lack the initial specificity, creating a contradiction in terms of enforcement. The definition of 'Competitive Business' relies on a complete definition of 'Competitive Products' which we've removed here.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Seller hereby agrees that, during the Restricted Period, except as permitted by Section 5 of this Agreement, it will not, directly or indirectly, own, manage, operate, control, invest in or acquire an interest in, or otherwise engage or participate in the establishment, management or operation of, any Competitive Business that sells or distributes Competitive Products, directly or indirectly, for ultimate purchase by consumers in the Protected Market, without regard to whether the Competitive Business has any office, manufacturing or other business facilities within the Protected Market.
perturbation.justification: The changed text removes the detailed definition of 'Competitive Products,' replacing it with a generic term. The contradicted text relies on the specific definition to define 'Competitive Business' and restrict the seller's activities. Without the detailed definition, the scope of the non-compete clause becomes ambiguous and unenforceable, directly conflicting with the intent and application of the contradicted section. The omission breaks the consistency and creates uncertainty, causing a strong in-text contradiction as described in the 'Omissions - In Text Contradiction' definition.

perturbation.original_text: (d) “ Infant and Toddler Retail Market ” shall mean those retail sales departments within retailers which sell products intended for children from ages zero to four years of age.
perturbation.changed_text: (d) “ Infant and Toddler Retail Market ” shall mean the retail market.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the age range (zero to four years of age) from the definition of 'Infant and Toddler Retail Market' makes the scope of the non-compete agreement ambiguous. Clauses referencing this market lack precise boundaries. This creates a contradiction when assessing whether a certain business activity falls within the restricted market. For instance, if 'Seller' begins designing for ages 5-7, does this violate section 2?
perturbation.contradicted_text: Seller hereby agrees that, during the Restricted Period, except as permitted by Section 5 of this Agreement, it will not, directly or indirectly, own, manage, operate, control, invest in or acquire an interest in, or otherwise engage or participate in the establishment, management or operation of, any Competitive Business that sells or distributes Competitive Products, directly or indirectly, for ultimate purchase by consumers in the Protected Market, without regard to whether the Competitive Business has any office, manufacturing or other business facilities within the Protected Market.
perturbation.justification: The original text defined 'Infant and Toddler Retail Market' as sales for children ages zero to four. The changed text broadens this definition to simply 'the retail market,' removing the age restriction. This directly contradicts the 'Contradicted Text,' which restricts the Seller from engaging in a 'Competitive Business' that sells 'Competitive Products' for purchase in the 'Protected Market'. Because the protected market is no longer clearly defined as products for ages 0-4, it creates ambiguity as to whether a business activity violates the agreement, thus causing a clear contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: (g) “ Protected Market ” shall mean the United States.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the definition of 'Protected Market,' enforcement of non-compete restrictions becomes impossible. Clauses prohibiting competition within the 'Protected Market' are now meaningless since the market isn't defined. This creates a fundamental in-text contradiction in applying the non-compete clauses. For example, section 2 mentions the term 'Protected Market', but it is now undefined.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Seller hereby agrees that, during the Restricted Period, except as permitted by Section 5 of this Agreement, it will not, directly or indirectly, own, manage, operate, control, invest in or acquire an interest in, or otherwise engage or participate in the establishment, management or operation of, any Competitive Business that sells or distributes Competitive Products, directly or indirectly, for ultimate purchase by consumers in the Protected Market, without regard to whether the Competitive Business has any office, manufacturing or other business facilities within the Protected Market.
perturbation.justification: The changed text removes the definition of "Protected Market", which is explicitly used in the contradicted text. The contradicted text states that the Seller agrees not to engage in any Competitive Business that sells products in the "Protected Market". Since the "Protected Market" is no longer defined, the clause is unenforceable, creating a direct contradiction and logical inconsistency.

perturbation.original_text: 2. No Competing Business . Seller hereby agrees that, during the Restricted Period, except as permitted by Section 5 of this Agreement, it will not, directly or indirectly, own, manage, operate, control, invest in or acquire an interest in, or otherwise engage or participate in the establishment, management or operation of, any Competitive Business that sells or distributes Competitive Products, directly or indirectly, for ultimate purchase by consumers in the Protected Market, without regard to whether the Competitive Business has any office, manufacturing or other business facilities within the Protected Market.
perturbation.changed_text: 2. No Competing Business . Seller hereby agrees that it will not engage in any business.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the non-compete clause from a restriction on 'Competitive Business' to a blanket prohibition on 'any business' is overly broad and likely unenforceable. It contradicts the exceptions outlined in Section 5 (Permitted Activities), rendering that section meaningless and internally contradictory.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Permitted Activities . The restrictions set forth in Sections 2, 3 and 4 of this Agreement shall not apply to Permitted Activities or to actions taken by Seller to the extent that such actions are expressly approved in writing by Purchaser.
perturbation.justification: The original text allowed the seller to engage in Competitive Business activities as permitted by Section 5. The changed text, however, broadly prohibits the seller from engaging in 'any business,' which directly contradicts the exceptions outlined in Section 5 ('Permitted Activities'). This contradiction arises because Section 5 explicitly allows for certain activities that would now be prohibited under the revised Section 2, rendering Section 5 meaningless and internally inconsistent.

perturbation.original_text: Seller acknowledges that (i) any violation of this Agreement will result in irreparable injury to Purchaser, (ii) damages at law would not be reasonable or adequate compensation to Purchaser for violation of this Agreement, and (iii) Purchaser shall be entitled to have the provisions of this Agreement specifically enforced by preliminary and permanent injunctive relief without the necessity of proving actual damages and without posting bond or other security as well as to an equitable accounting of all earnings, profits and other benefits arising out of any such violation.
perturbation.changed_text: Seller acknowledges that Purchaser shall be entitled to damages arising out of any violation.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the clause stating that 'damages at law would not be reasonable or adequate compensation' undermines the basis for injunctive relief. This omission suggests that monetary damages are sufficient, contradicting the later claim that injunctive relief is warranted. The initial agreement states (ii) damages at law would not be reasonable or adequate compensation to Purchaser for violation of this Agreement, this statment is been directly contradicted by the new change.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Seller acknowledges that Purchaser shall be entitled to damages arising out of any violation.
perturbation.justification: The original text states that 'damages at law would not be reasonable or adequate compensation,' supporting the need for injunctive relief. The changed text, by simply stating 'Purchaser shall be entitled to damages,' implies that damages *are* sufficient, directly contradicting the basis for injunctive relief outlined elsewhere and creating a conflict. The contradicted text says 'Seller acknowledges that Purchaser shall be entitled to damages arising out of any violation', while the original agreement that the new text is meant to replace (ii) damages at law would not be reasonable or adequate compensation to Purchaser for violation of this Agreement. Thus, the new text directly contradicts the original intention, and thus, creating a contradiction within the document.
